planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose colonies and is best left undisturbed once established.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral woodland soil in partial to full shade; false Solomon's seal spreads slowly by rhizomes to form loose colonies and is best left undisturbed once established. Maianthemum racemosum (formerly Smilacina racemosa) is one of the most beautiful and versatile native woodland wildflowers of eastern North America; it produces arching stems bearing large, parallel-veined, oval leaves arranged alternately in a pattern nearly identical to true Solomon's seal (Polygonatum), but unlike Solomon's seal it bears showy, frothy plumes of tiny, fragrant, white flowers at the stem tips in late spring — one of the most ornamental flowering effects in the spring shade garden — followed by clusters of ruby-red berries in late summer that are mildly edible when fully ripe but somewhat cathartic. It is an outstanding, all-season shade perennial with interest from spring flowers through red fall berries and golden fall foliage.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; starry false Solomon's seal forms spreading colonies by rhizomes and is more adaptable to dry shade and sandy soils than its larger relative M. racemosum.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; starry false Solomon's seal forms spreading colonies by rhizomes and is more adaptable to dry shade and sandy soils than its larger relative M. racemosum. Maianthemum stellatum (formerly Smilacina stellata) is a delicate, smaller relative of false Solomon's seal found across a wide range of North American habitats from moist woodlands to sand dunes and dry prairies; it has slender, arching stems with narrower, clasping leaves and bears loose racemes of 5–15 star-shaped, white flowers with 6 distinct petals in mid-spring, followed by greenish berries that turn dark red-striped then black as they ripen in late summer. It is among the most adaptable of native woodland perennials, tolerating a wider range of soil conditions than most shade plants, and makes an excellent naturalizing groundcover under deciduous or mixed-conifer trees in zones 3–8.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; skeleton flower absolutely requires consistent moisture and good drainage — it will not tolerate drought or waterlogging and grows best in cool, north-facing shaded sites.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; skeleton flower absolutely requires consistent moisture and good drainage — it will not tolerate drought or waterlogging and grows best in cool, north-facing shaded sites. Diphylleia grayi is a Japanese mountain woodland perennial that has become increasingly popular for its unique optical phenomenon — its white, waxy petals turn completely transparent when wet from rain, revealing the delicate venation of the petals and earning it the name "skeleton flower"; the large, deeply lobed, peltate leaves superficially resemble umbrella plant or mayapple, and the white flowers are borne in cymes at the leaf axils in late spring. After flowering, it produces striking, deep-blue, grape-like berries on red stems in summer that are the plant's second season of ornamental interest. It demands consistent cool moisture and is challenging but rewarding for shade gardeners in zones 5–8.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions with care in fall in moist, humus-rich, cool, acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; Indian cucumber root is notoriously difficult to establish from divisions and is very slow-growing, but it is a uniquely beautiful native wildflower worth the patience and should only be purchased from specialist nurseries.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions with care in fall in moist, humus-rich, cool, acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; Indian cucumber root is notoriously difficult to establish from divisions and is very slow-growing, but it is a uniquely beautiful native wildflower worth the patience and should only be purchased from specialist nurseries. Medeola virginiana is a slender, elegant eastern North American native with two whorls of leaves arranged like tiers on a single stem — a lower whorl of 5–9 leaves midway up the stem and an upper whorl of 3 leaves at the summit — producing pendant, small, greenish-yellow, lily-like flowers from the upper whorl in early summer followed by striking clusters of deep purple-black berries on bright red pedicels in fall that are the plant's most ornamental feature. The rhizome tastes like cucumber and was used as food by Native Americans (giving the plant its common name), though digging it kills the plant; it is best appreciated as a living wildflower. It is a colony-forming species that spreads very slowly in undisturbed woodland.

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; red baneberry is easily grown in any cool, moist, shaded site and spreads slowly by rhizomes and self-seeding to form handsome clumps over time.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial to full shade; red baneberry is easily grown in any cool, moist, shaded site and spreads slowly by rhizomes and self-seeding to form handsome clumps over time. Actaea rubra is a native North American woodland perennial in the buttercup family with large, compound, twice-ternate leaves that give it a delicate, airy texture; in mid-spring it produces fluffy, white bottle-brush-like flower clusters on leafy stems, followed in summer by brilliant, glossy, red (occasionally white in form neglecta) berries in compact clusters on red pedicels that are among the most ornamental of any native woodland fruit but are highly toxic — all parts of the plant are poisonous. It is an excellent companion for black cohosh, white baneberry, ferns, and hostas in shaded woodland gardens; the red berries are eagerly eaten by birds and provide important late-summer wildlife food.

common_name: Canada Mayflower (Wild Lily of the Valley)

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions or container plants in fall in moist, humus-rich, cool, strongly to moderately acidic soil in partial to full shade; Canada mayflower spreads readily by rhizomes to form dense, low colonies under conifers and is an excellent groundcover in cool-zone shade gardens.

directions: Plant rhizome divisions or container plants in fall in moist, humus-rich, cool, strongly to moderately acidic soil in partial to full shade; Canada mayflower spreads readily by rhizomes to form dense, low colonies under conifers and is an excellent groundcover in cool-zone shade gardens. Maianthemum canadense is one of the most abundant and adaptable native woodland groundcovers of the boreal and northern hardwood forests of North America; a diminutive, 3–6-inch-tall perennial with 1–3 heart-shaped, shiny, green leaves and delicate, racemes of tiny, white, 4-petaled flowers in late spring followed by pale red-speckled berries ripening to solid red in late summer, providing abundant food for hermit thrushes and other forest birds. It forms aggressive colonies in cool, acidic, conifer-shaded sites (pH 4.5–5.5) where few other plants thrive, making it an indispensable groundcover in pine and spruce understory gardens in zones 2–6; it is less successful in warm southern climates.
